questions.question: What is the role of the control plane in hybrid quantum networks?

options.text: Make routing decisions and manage resources using classical processing

questions.explanation: The control plane uses classical processing to make decisions about routing, resource allocation, and session setup. It tells the quantum data plane what to do but doesn't directly handle quantum states.

questions.question: What is the main benefit of Software-Defined Quantum Networking (SDQN)?

options.text: Centralized control with global network view and programmability

questions.explanation: SDQN provides centralized control that has a global view of the network, enabling optimal routing and resource allocation. The programmable interface allows flexible configuration of quantum network elements.

questions.question: In the overlay integration pattern, how does the quantum network relate to classical?

options.text: Quantum uses classical network for control but operates independently
Classical runs on top of quantum
They share the same physical infrastructure

questions.explanation: In the overlay model, the quantum network uses the classical network for control plane functions (routing decisions, signaling) but the quantum data plane operates independently for carrying quantum states.

questions.question: Why is timing synchronization critical in hybrid architectures?

options.text: Quantum operations require precise timing and control must not add latency

questions.explanation: Quantum operations (entanglement generation, swapping, detection) require precise timing. Control decisions must be made fast enough that control plane latency doesn't cause quantum states to decohere.

questions.question: What is a hybrid service in quantum networking?

options.text: A service that uses only quantum communication
A service combining quantum and classical network capabilities
A service that works on multiple operating systems

questions.explanation: A hybrid service combines both quantum capabilities (like QKD for key distribution) and classical capabilities (like authenticated channels for key exchange protocols) to provide a complete solution.

questions.question: Why is controller redundancy important in SDN architectures?

options.text: To avoid a single point of failure in network control

questions.explanation: A centralized SDN controller is a single point of failure - if it fails, the network cannot adapt or reconfigure. Redundant controllers ensure continued operation even when one fails.

questions.question: In hybrid architectures, quantum operations can be fully controlled by the classical control plane in real-time.
questions.explanation: False. While the control plane makes high-level decisions, quantum operations often happen too fast for real-time classical control. Local hardware must handle timing-critical operations; the control plane sets up configurations in advance.
